Class was interesting, but the material was difficult. Craver is very smart and gives interesting lectures, but sometimes he goes on long unrelated tangents which are hard to follow if you aren't well verse in the topic. A bit disorganized.
I'm sure he's a great person, but Craver isn't the best professor. He often is disorganized in and out of class, to the detriment of his students.

Dr. Craver is an incredibly interesting and likeable professor.  His lectures are intriguing and he often encourages class discussion.  The class material is difficult to engage in since it is mostly independent,however Dr. Craver is approachable for help.
An easy going professor who really teaches you how to think. Professor Craver may be a little disorganized, but a great, helpful person to get to know

craver is an incredibly funny, laid-back guy; great to have as a friend, but he does not belong in the classroom.  our tests contained material we never could have guessed would have been on there, and he was extremely disorganized and wasted precious class time--i don't recommend him.

Dr. Craver is creative, good at inspiring students, good at both lecturing and listening, incredibly helpful, funny, frighteningly intelligent, and an amazing professor.  Teaches a very hard class, but is a fair grader.

Hamilton gives pretty bland, borderline monotone lectures. He's a bit of an awkward fellow, but not in the charming way, more in the sit-in-silence-for-30-seconds-while-he-waits-for-someone-to-react-to-his-joke way. I wouldn't say I learned anything from the class. Assignment expectations are arbitrary and at times unreasonable. Easy grader though.

Took Hamilton for an online class and it has been the worst experience of my life. He posted the wrong syllabus and did not inform anyone when he corrected it. He reused last semester's recorded lectures but didn't notify us there was incorrect info included. Everything is unclear, esp. due dates., and there is little context given for assignments.

In my 4 years at WashU, the worst professor I've had by a pretty large margin. He has serious ADHD during class and will change topics frequently, and not in a good way. Grading is erratic and makes no sense. Grades very poorly if you disagree with his uber-liberal views. Avoid at all costs. Seriously, you're reading this website for reviews. Avoid
Good teacher. Speak up in class and go to office hours and you will get an A...otherwise, you should not be at Wash U.
Very interesting guy. Don't really need to do the readings, just make sure you go to office hours and show that you care. Be willing to talk in and out of class with him and you'll get an A.

Hamilton is the best.  He's totally into the material and while  he is kind of hard to follow and, if you stick with him you'll learn to love poetry and he'll help you improve as a writer.  Not the  best for someone who wants everything laid out for them, he leaves a lot of flexibility in his assignments.

Ham is an ***hole. Thinks he knows everything, extremely judgmental about your writing if you don't agree with his opinions-- this seems to be common among non-fiction writing profs. Very harsh grader, and outright mean sometimes. I hated going to class. I worked my butt off and met with him about my papers, and still didn't get the grade I deserve

Worst teacher I've had at Wash U. He spends a huge amount of time going over elementary, repetitive examples from the textbook. Lots of awkward pauses in class. Feels the need to write everything on the chalkboard instead of just discuss it with the class. Harsh grader. Completely unorganized and could never seem to answer questions.
His discussion style is very convoluted and while there isn't a doubt that he's very smart, he isn't very helpful in my opinion.
Extremely smart, personable, great discussion leader, will really help you to grow your thinking/writing abilities...  A+++++++
Professor Hamilton is by far the best writing professor at Wash U.  I learned so much from him.
Had him over the summer for FSAP. What a great guy. Fair grader, helpful, approachable, etc. Can get sidetracked in class discussions though.
He is very cocerned and understanding. Not too hard. Class discussions can get boring but generally pretty laid back.
He is fantastic!

Kirby is the worst professor I have ever had. He has unrealistic expectations and has encouraged us to "find" objects to use from restaurants and places near us. Which just means steal. He has been downright rude to people and makes fun of students. He likes when we tear each other down in Crit. He needs a reality check

Pros: pushes you to explore new materials provides lots of opportunities (exhibitions, conventions) actually cares about teaching you to be an artist life skills you will make pieces that you are proud of Cons: Beware, his mood can change quickly will push his own opinions on your piece Advice: Be steadfast, independent, and hardworking.

Avoid at all costs. Professor is out of touch with his students, pretty much manipulating them to make what he wants, instead of what actually interests the students. Unrealistic expectations, with very vague project guidelines. This class was a waste of time and overall made me frustrated.

I lovveeee kirby; he is an inspiring proffessor. many people critize him for the difficulty of the work and his open ended assignments but that is why i liked him. he pushed us to try new media like welding and wood work - things other 3D classes didnt do. i love his sense of humor he brings to class. overall, i would love to take his class again

Noah really inspired me. He's a really charismatic instructor and kept the class from being boring. Working 3D is hard, but he really knows his stuff and I feel like I learned so much from him. Be prepared to work hard and think, but you'll feel really proud of yourself when you see what you can do! You'll do things you never thought you could

Mainly wanted to put in a good word for him: don't be scared of his review! He is more clear than other professors and offers a lot of resources to help you with your projects. His assignments are also good for portfolio pieces. He makes you make a lot of models before making the real thing: important learning tools! Cares about students' learning

Noah cares about his students, but he's just not that inspiring. He wants us to succeed, and I like him a lot for that. He tries to make class fun, but it is hard to teach 3D design to kids who are interested in Communication Design. Kirby does try his best and gives good feedback, but it would be better if he gave it earlier rather than later.

Noah Kirby is an awful person and professor. He forces students to be ruthless in critique by rewarding them for tearing their peers down. Would not recommend, he made me want to drop out of the art school and made me hate sculpture/3D work, things I had been fond of in the past.

He is a very strong artist.  I took blacksmithing, and yes you will be outside. You will work hard, and you will learn a lot. Noah doesn't go and tell people what to do, but if you ask him he will help. He critiques everyone's work in order to push them to do their best. I poured hours into the class, learned a lot, and had a great time.

Noah states he is an artist first, a teacher second. You are not a student - you are his competition. So he has no interest in helping you succeed - if so his life as an artist would be exponentially more difficult. Zero sense of art history. He will use his rank to his own advantage and grade low. Not recommended.

Noah is very helpful if you know the right way to ask for help.  A lot of times he's going to leave things open ended and you might be confused, but I think overall that's a good thing and gets you thinking.  He expects you to be very independent. He's clearly passionate about art, and is great to have one-on-on studio visits with.

I had a lot of fun with Noah. He's a great guy if you try to understand him. He's not going to give you a lot of hints but that's what art school is about-figuring things out for yourself. I wasn't happy with my grade but I really enjoyed the class overall.

Noah made the class a challenge.  He was reluctant to answer questions in any kind of detail, which often left me confused, but also taught me to think and make decisions about my work on my own.  Noah was a good instructor overall, although I didn't always enjoy the class when I was in it.

The class was really physically demanding.  I learned a lot but didn't receive any feedback during the semester.  At the end I was given a pretty harsh grade that I don't think reflected my effort.  Don't take it if you don't want to spend hours in freezing rain blacksmithing.

Don't take a class with Dr. Ferrer unless you feel comfortable teaching yourself the content.  He frequently came to office hours without looking at the homework he assigned, so when you ask questions he says he doesn't know because he hasn't looked at it yet.

Ferrer's lecture were messy and it honestly  didn't seem like he came prepared for lectures, as he would bounce around topics and do problems incorrectly which made lectures very confusing. I took Physics 192, the next Physics course, with another professor that also teaches 191 and she was vastly superior to Ferrer. Nice person, bad prof.

He does a good job of making the lecture engaging and interactive (which is pretty hard with physics) but he usually runs out of time or does the problem wrong so it can get confusing. It is a tough class but if you take advantage of the resources like PLTL you can do good.
Ferrer thinks that everyone in his class understands physics already, and he goes over his examples very quickly. You will have to do a lot of self-teaching in this class, so be prepared.

He's got to be the worst prof I've had at WashU. I don't think he realizes that he's teaching to students with little to no physics knowledge along with the ones who've taken physics before. He's so bad at explaining things, so I had to watch the other lecturers' lectures to feel like I stood a chance when it came time to take the exams.
This guy is an absolute genius. Great physics professor

Ferrer is a really funny teacher w good jokes and makes class go by so fast. Does a lot of proofs but will do examples if you ask him to. Neat accent. Does a lot of in class demonstrations and makes them really fun. Will call on random people but doesn't destroy you if you are wrong like some professors. Overall entertaining and informative class

Ferrer is funny and he is willing to explain concepts thoroughly if you have questions. Beware. If you sit in the back, he's more likely to call on you if you don't raise your hand. The class itself is a bit of work due to daily and weekly homework assignments.
Great lecturer. Doesn't much budge on grading however.
He is quiet unclear about his lectures, he is pretty bad that take away your attendance score easily, he even makes the Mid Term 1 the hardest ever happened in WUSTL history!!!!

Professor Ferrer is the man.  Always makes class fun, keeps lectures interesting with numerous 2-minute problems.  Has a very good demeanor when discussing in class about the mentioned problems.  Although the problem sets are tedious and the textbook is way overused, this is not his fault.  It is how the course is designed.  As a teacher, he rocks!

Material can be very difficult but Ferrer is clear and intelligible in his math.  Problem sets are extremely time intensive and involve an understanding that goes way beyond lecture.  Exam difficulty varied from absolutely impossible to pretty easy so you really have to be prepped for anything.

Had him for mechanics spring 2011. He would cover material in class only after he had assigned and collected homework on it, so class was pretty useless. I ended up reading the textbook cover-to-cover, and the class was still hard. Exam questions came completely out of left field.

He's got a cool Spanish accent.  His lectures focus on proofs rather than example problems, which is a problem, because his problem sets have extremely difficult problems that the textbook can't help with.  An easy problem set is one that can be done without any outside help.  Exams range from impossible to manageable.

Great guy. Very caring about students, has a sense of humor too. Lectures can be a little unstructured which makes it hard if you're not following along but if you put in some effort, you'll get a lot out of it. At the same time, he's a pretty easy grader so if you don't you'll probably be fine too.
Infante is the best professor I have taken at WashU. He is one of the nicest guys ever and grades very nicely as well. I'm pretty sure everyone in his class ends up with an A.

Very kind and concerned teacher - he cares about you a lot and makes great strides to look out for the class, including extending deadlines quite often. I am naturally pretty good at writing but it seemed like pretty much everyone was getting an A for each essay. You can participate and write well in this class without reading each book in detail.

Professor Infante is the best professor I have ever interacted with. He is understanding, the work load is very manageable, and he is a genuinely kind (and unbelievably smart) person. If you can take a class with him you have to, he encourages the exploration of literature in a way that you wont find elsewhere.

Prof Infante is such a nice and genuinely caring person. Despite the fact that I never understood a single reading and could probably, at the end of the semester, not tell you what this class is about I would take the course again in a heartbeat. It is rare you find a professor as nice and class discussion was always fun even when it was confusing!
Great teacher and nice person. Readings can be a bit existential and hard to understand, but grading is not too hard.

Wonderful man! His World Lit class made me think about stuff I have never pondered before. His class is very discussion-based, and I learned so much through this style of learning. I would love to take another class with him-- very enthusiastic, easy class, amazing professor!! Cares about his students a lot. Take World Lit with him if you can!!!

Prof. Infante is super enthusiastic, kind, and reasonable. World Lit challenged my thinking but was never too stressful, and he both gave great lectures and made sure the discussions went somewhere. You can tell that he loves the subject matter, and he is really willing to help with papers. If you put in effort, your worldview will expand a lot!

Professor Infante is great! World Lit is a really interesting class and he clearly is very passionate about it. He chose some really great texts and always gave insightful lectures. He is very laid back and approachable, enthusiastic about what he teaches, and is a pretty easy grader. Overall it is a very casual atmosphere and highly enjoyable.

He's a G. First day of class he played the Pixies, I've never met anybody with a more impressive name than Ignacio Infante, his accent is infinitely dope,  he's just a funny and laid back guy. And he definitely knows his shtuff. Essays aren't too bad, I liked the books but you didn't have to read carefully at all if you pay attention in class
Prof. Infante is a wonderful professor, very engaging and fun to listen to.  He genuinely cares about his students and wants them to succeed.  Awesome teacher!
World Lit was an amazing class!!! Ignacio is the best. Take every class you can with him!
World Literature with Professor Infante was a great introductory class. The works he picked for us to read and view were, for the most part, very interesting.

Prof Infante is both an adorable person and a fabulous professor.  He listens to his students and makes time to meet at least once during the semester with each student in the class. He make required material interesting and understandable. I furthered my Spanish significantly in the 2 semesters in his classes. SPAN 307 & 334.

Assignments were easy but she gave such specific grades (i.e. 13.6/15) and she comes off a little passive aggressive when asking for extensions. I missed a few classes due to illness and she only excused a few of them (1 was the class I missed before I registered for it) which dropped my grade. Take her class if you have to but be prepared.

Professor Berg really cares for her students and is very much there for them. She is a very fair grader and participation does matter, but also she knows how to make a class fun if you are open and willing in class. Definitely would take again. She's the type of professor to ask you how you are doing outside of class.

Some of the lectures could be a little boring. Nancy's anti-humor was entertaining at times. Very unclear instructions at times, but Prof Berg does really care about her students and overall I enjoyed the class.

Dr. Berg is quite awkward and, at times, difficult to have as a teacher. Her instructions can be quite ambiguous and there's a gap between what she wants and what we understand. However, it was clear by the way she weighed work, encouraged students, and listened to us that she cared about our success. Also, I promise you'll warm up to her humor.

Prof. Berg is a very sweet lady who puts effort into making connections with her students. She is great at facilitating discussion, sometimes lengthening segments when the discussion is rich, and open to moving on when it's lacking. She is certainly not closed-minded... she is really good at bringing multiple sides to controversial issues.

Incredibly helpful and concerned about students.  Sets high expectations for writing.  Did not find her closed minded at all and think she actually allowed us to discuss the readings however we wished, though after clarifications. Again, SUPER helpful and concerned for students.
I really enjoyed the class - prof berg was helpful outside of class and i thought my writing and reading improved. it was also fun - take lit of childhood if it's offered again.

Most boring a comparative literature class could possibly be. Most discussion was about what happened in the books we were reading. Very superficial discussion. As a person, professor seemed cold and uninterested. Overall very awkward experience. One of the worst classes I've taken at WU.

I took this class despite ominous comments; I wish I had listened. The literary discussions fail to sustain my interest or go past the surface of the text. The class often feels like a plot review. When a comment is made, she will often disagree, and does not let a discussion develop, instead letting comments hang in the air or objecting to them.

Did not find her closeminded. Found that she opened my eyes to new ways of approaching the topic/reading. I can see how someone very set in their ways would feel snubbed, but she will always listen, she just won't necessarily agree. What fun would class be if everyone agreed, though? Definitely recommended.
Close minded in a University which is known for its open intellectual exchanges.
Definitely comes into class with a set agenda, and does not want to hear an opposing opinions.  Rather close minded and difficult to approach.
No Comments

Professor Cummins is the best professor I have ever had.  The man is a genius.  There is not a single question he can't answer.  He assigns a lot of reading (200-300 pages a class). At the beginning of the semester hes a really hard grader, but his final grades are always higher than the essay averages. He takes care of you if you put in the work.

although this class is an intense amount of work as a freshman, i don't regret taking it at all! if you have any interest AT ALL in law or constitutional issues, take this class!!! it was so helpful and cummins is so sweet and really wants you to do well!
Makes you work hard, but well worth it. Class is rewarding, and interesting. Very helpful and cares about students.
I've taken lots of classes with Cummins. He expects a ton but it's worthwhile.  He is really really nice.  Expect lots of reading.
By far, the best class I took at WashU.  Amazing professor, but a LOT of work.  Expects a lot, but again, the best class I took!  His class is the reason I decided to attend law school.

By far the best class I took as a freshman. The class is very interesting, and Cummins is a great professor and advisor. The class is a ton of work, but in the end, it's completely worth it. Over the semester, you can notice a substantial progression in the quality of your work, and an A is possible
Best class I have taken at Wash. U.  Take it, take him, he's awesome.
Great guy, good teacher.  The class is work-intensive, but student effort is rewarded and very rewarding as well.

amazing prof, and great guy too.  his classes are more work-intensive than any others at wash u, but it is worth it because you learn so much.  although the work is intense, you can still get a good grade as long as you do it.

Cummins is da man!! He's scary at first due to the amount of work he gives you, but you end up learning so much and he gets easier as the semester goes on. Take a class from him! He is what is remaining of Wash U's sociology dept.

Emily Guignon is the best French teacher I've ever had.  She has an incredible flair for making texts, vocab, and even verbs memorable, but you have to do the work of studying yourself. Guignon's teaching style is confused and disorganized. I agree that she jumps from topic to topic, and an hour class seems to drag on for forever. Tests and quizzes aren't too hard though, but the work becomes tedious, and she is not very helpful. From what I here, it's also really hard to meet with her outside of class.
I stared at the clock the whole time. Such a boring class, in my opinion. Other people seemed to like her a little better than I did, though. (I accidentally posted this under 307, but I took 201.)
I stared at the clock the whole time. Such a boring class, in my opinion. (Other people seemed to like her a little better than I did, though.)

Guignon is continuously aggravatingly disorganized and tends to jumps from one subject to another while diverging on various unrelated tangents every 5 minutes. She knows her stuff very well though and is usually exceedingly helpful if you ask questions, especially if you stay after or go to her hours.
Mme Guignon is super disorganized and we rarely stuck to the syllabus, but otherwise she's very nice and sweet.

This teacher was the most disorganized one I have had in four years at Wash U.  She never had a class plan, which made us fall behind often, but then she would try to catch up to the syllabus, meanwhile leaving entire acts of plays and entire poems undiscussed after they were assigned readings.  It was REALLY frustrating. Not recommended.
awesome

Love her! She's exciting during class and makes it fun and enjoyable. Very relaxing and conversational. Her quizzes are a little tough sometimes, but there are so many, it's easy to get a decent grade. I'm excited to take 201 with her.

Guignon, while a very nice person, is the most disorganized prof. I've had so far at Wash U. She forgets to bring quizzes on scheduled quiz days (so she makes them up on the spot) and she has been known to misplace papers and tests. Her tests are very difficult, particularly the final.

She is super approachable and really helpful. Class sometimes got a little boring, but I think that was more the nature of the class than her. I really enjoyed having her and look forward to having her again.
Hard tests.
She has a bad reputation but I enjoyed her class as well as getting to know her.  She invited all of her 307 students over for dinner at the end of the semester which was nice.

she is seriously SO sweet. a little disorganized, maybe, but the sweetest woman ever. she's just so nice! i feel like no professors here are like that. and she's a great teacher. it's not one of those classes where you stare at the clock the whole time. you won't regret taking her classes.
Readings and class discussion not so interesting, but grades fairly.
She's really a great teacher. Classes are alot of fun. Watch out though, tough grader on written work.
Good professor.  A little bit more work than expected, papers were graded pretty hard.  Other than that look for a professor who's nice and chill but not afraid to be a stickler when you slack off
Challenging
She is an excellent and informative teacher who makes the classes interesting and easy to learn
She challenged me
Great film professor...fun WWII movies and not to much work

Shes spunky.  Lots of energy and very enthusiastic.  Sometimes more of a stickler than you would expect, but overall a highly recommended course. small groupwork can get repeditive and irritating.
She's great, really  helpful and I really enjoyed the class.
She was a great film prof, even though she is in the German Dept.  I hope she keeps teaching film classes!

She provided excellent insights, and gave us great reading materials, but there was too much small group time.  I would have liked some variation in that regard, but the class was great and highly recommended.
Friendly and usually interesting.  Very reasonable person.
sucked

I found it very difficult to follow explanations from the instructor. I was able to get by during the first two units based on knowledge I had gained from my AP Physics class in high school. Unclear explanations and mistakes in problems by Professor Gibbons left the class reserved and nervous about answering questions. The book was not helpful.

I sympathize with Professor Gibbons, but he is a little too old to teach. He is really not clear, sometimes he can't even understand our questions. The course is easy enough so it wasn't a total disaster. But for anyone without prior physics knowledge I suggest you find another section for 197 ASAP, because u will be stuck with him for 198 as well.
Good teacher, friendly and available. Class is both easy and interesting. Does not take attendance. I don't see why everyone finds his class so boring, I had fun.

Solar System Astronomy: worst class ever with him. it is a 100 person class but only 15 people show up (im sadly one of them) because he's so boring. Also, it is supposed to be an easy math course but it turns into intermediate physics quite quickly. DO NOT TAKE THIS CLASS
very helpful in the lab
physics 117 is just plain hard, and many non-physics people take it. don't let those ratings change your opinion   he's a great teacher, knows a ton about electronics, and he smells funny

Good lecturer and knowledgeable about the material. Wants proofs done his way. Success in his class feels rewarding. Publicly berated a student for coughing during lecture and gave death stares the rest of the semester per cough - don't sneeze/cough even with a mask on. Impersonable but strong academic. Take the class pass/fail or not at all.

Professor Stern is fantastic! His lectures are fast but easy to follow and delivered with enthusiasm. Don't be afraid to ask questions during class. He is tremendously helpful in office hours, too. So if you are stumped on a homework problem - and you will be - show up and he will clear up anything that's puzzling you. And he's a metalhead \m/ !

This class was ridiculously hard for the type of material covered. Professor Stern stood in front of the board reading the textbook proofs to us, answering no questions, and then expecting us to be capable of the hardest problems on WebWork and Crowdmark. Worst professor ever. Failed the final after getting As all semester. Do not take.

Wow, this guy was bad at his job. Made my life awful for a semester. Horribly difficult homework and bad teaching don't mix well, but you will have to learn to cope in any Stern class. Showed up to every single class, did every homework, and went to review sessions but was lost the whole time and didn't learn a thing. Hard not to hate this guy.

Stern is by far one of the worst professors that I have taken at WashU. Every class day, you will spend the entire hour rushing to copy notes while retaining no knowledge. His lectures are almost entirely theoretical and he will spend so much time writing down tedious proofs that have no application for homework, recitation, and exams.

Ari Stern is the worst professor I've ever had. He spends the majority of his lectures on useless proofs and theoretical concepts and then only does a few incredibly basic and unhelpful problems. The WebWork and Crowdmark are far more difficult than anything in class, which makes them much more difficult to do than should be necessary for Calc 3.

Professor Stern is a great lecturer. I struggle with math, but I've been able to stay engaged and understand his lectures. He does a really great job explaining and providing different ways to visualize difficult concepts. You'll need to study a lot outside of class, since it is a tough subject, but Prof. Stern makes it more approachable.

I LOVED calculus until I took it with Stern. Don't waste your time taking it with Stern because you'll need to teach yourself the material anyway. His lectures are messy and very theoretical so you won't even get any formulas needed to work through the problems. It is also nearly impossible to hear him because he always speaks facing the board.

Awful professor. Goes way too fast in lecture, and makes the tests insanely hard. The averages in Calc 3 this semester speak for themselves, and show that he is truly not up to par. Better off watching review videos on YouTube than wasting your time in lecture.

Stern doesn't teach concepts very well, and also doesn't go over very many examples in class. Definitely a quantity over quality kind of teacher. Even in WashU's lacking math department, he stands out as significantly below average.
The math department is lucky to have him. Stern is a fantastic and devoted teacher
He is one of best professors I ever had. He is young, smart, and cool. His lecture is never boring.

So so so helpful. I had already taken this class in high school, but just wanted to make sure I had a solid base before moving on to calc 2. Super nice, always helpful at office hours, and doesn't get frustrated even if you have dumb questions. His tests are pretty easy if you study, he doesn't try to confuse you or give you unnecessarily hard qs.
i heard that six out of all six students in japanese level 4 class dropped out in the 1st semester.

i don't recommend taking japanese class with him. not only is he stubborn and old-school minded, but he grades extremely hard. i heard six out of six ppl dropped out in the first semester of japanese level 3 class last semester.
A lot of work and very hard to get a good grade. He expects a lot from you. Just had a oral presentation and he is very strict and uptight. Better to take the class with another professor.

If you want to have a high cumulative gpa, do NOT take the class with him. He is the kind of guy who enjoys and feels satisfied giving you loads of work. He will at times ridicule and give sarcastic comments directly to you. He is also very old-school minded and won't even allow you wear a hat to class. Expect loads of work.
Great Instructor, hard class, but I learn a lot.  Wash U has a great professor!
its too bad he doesn't teach first level anymore.
Aridome is great!
He was a great addition to the Japanese Program.  WashU should hold on to him.
the best professor ive ever had, no joke.  take his course if you can!
great addition to Wash U staff. He has the energy to keep you going in a very hard enviroment. He really sees it as his responsibility to help you succeed
Sometimes difficult, but often willing to keep working with you.  Has a great sense of humor and always keeps the class fun and serious simultaneously.
His lectures are hard to follow at times but he's getting better. Friendly, knowledgeable, funny, and helpful.
nice guy but his humor is hard to understand

soo easy...i never go to class and still do well because everything is in the book. the person before is right, the tests are exactly the suggested exercises, never knew my college calc class would be easier than my high school math class.

Rediculously easy, basically every question on the test is listed in the "suggested exercises," which have answers at the back of the book, btw.  not the most enthusiastic person, true, but sincere, and AMAZING compared to Friewald!!  i looove his class (whenever i don't skip.. which isn't a problem, it's all in the book). helpful, nice old booger.

I was dreading this required course but he made sure we understood every important topic before moving on.  Great teacher!

Wang LaoShi is the best! That's all.

If you have taken a class in the Chinese department at WashU, you know what you're in for. There is a lot of work, but every Professor I have taken is an amazing instructor, including Wang Laoshi. The lessons in this course a little bit longer with more vocabulary, but you will learn a lot. Study the textbook thoroughly, and you will do fine.

Had Liang Laoshi before and the Chinese dept seems on top of their game. Wang Laoshi was awesome. Class was a lot of work but very rewarding. 4 people in my class so my Chinese improved a lot due to good class structure and Wang's office hours. Recitations also helpful. Make some Chinese native friends and go to the Chinese dept events for fluency!

I just finished Chinese 360 with wang laoshi. Not gonna lie, at the beginning, it was super hard for me(compared to ap Chinese) and I considered dropping but if you review every day, you can be very successful in the class. 

She's super helpful in office hours and encouraged me to keep going, and I've seen so much improvement so thx  wang laoshi!

Wang Laoshi is a great professor. She can be a little standoffish when you first meet her but she warms up and is very kind. She is very accessible and always gives good feedback. Participation is important.

Wang is an incredible teacher. She's super kind, patient, understanding, welcoming, and accessible. Canvas page could be a little better organized, but she follows the semester plan and syllabus exactly so you know what to expect. Attendance and participation are super important, but if you do the work it's not hard to do well.

Really, really appreciate Wang Laoshi. Grew on me a ton as the semester went on. She is very professional but overtime opened up more and clearly really cares about her students. She is very patient and a great teacher, and was pretty lenient on the students who were obviously less invested in the class. Also not that hard to get an A if u show up.
wang laoshi is literally the sweetest

<3 <3 I had Wang Laoshi for my first chinese class at WashU and was not disappointed. She is super caring, personable, and since the classes are small, all of her students a have gotten really close with her. The class difficulty is fitting for the level, and if you are ready to take a 300 level chinese class, she is the teacher to have for sure.

Wow wow wow, what an amazing laoshi! She is so kind and really wants you to know the content. As long as you put in effort and show it, you will get a good grade. She is very forgiving and makes it clear that learning is the only goal. Take any class with her and you won't be disappointed!
She doesnt require too many assignments done because its mostly studying and showing what you know. Shes great at clarifying and slowing down when youre confused.
Really dry lecturer, his slides/lectures were also really hard to understand (super hard to discern what's important vs. anecdotal details). Not a super tough class to do well in, though.

Pretty poor lectures -- really hard to understand what is a key point and what is a random detail. The quizzes are similar -- he chooses to put some random details on them that he doesn't really talk about in class. Not super engaging, wouldn't take again.

The course was very interesting, and Dr. Boyer was friendly. However, lectures were disorganized and convoluted. Clarifications were often even more confusing. Initially released the wrong grades for everyone at the end of the year, and still hasn't published midterm grades. Overall, an unnecessarily stressful experience.
Seems to take satisfaction from bullying his students, and treats struggling students with contempt.  Should not be a professor.
Egomaniac
Dr. Boyer is an amazing professor. He can be a little disorganized at times, and sometimes his explanations can be confusing, but overall there is more pros than cons.

Dr. Boyer did not adequately communicate the expectations of the course. The class was graded mostly on group projects and participation. Lectures were boring, and discussion was awkward. Boyer didn't have regular office hours and was very hard to reach. Would not recommend this course.

Professor Boyer is a nice enough guy, but is unprofessional and his actions suggest he does not care for students or for the course. Had him for a writing intensive course. Thorough and well organized. Let the class out early often.

Professor Copeland is not only an excellent professor, but also a REALLY kind person! She gives clear and interesting lectures, and always response emails in time. Prof Copeland is very knowledgable on Japanese culture and always tells humorous stories/tales. She is just amazing. Would definitely take her class again!

Dr. Rebcca Coepland is an awesome teacher! She gives amazing lectures and engages the class with her lessons and content. Her assignments can be graded a little harshly, but if you put effort in, you'll be totally fine. She clearly communicates her expectations for her assignments. She is also super caring and very funny. Totally take her classes!

Professor Copeland is the best! Not only is she highly knowledgable but her lectures and class discussions are always so interesting. Plus, she is accessible and very responsive to students. I am planning to take another one of her classes again before I graduate.
Professor Copeland is great. She's a really nice lady, and her class is really interesting and engaging. Highly recommend her class on Japanese Civilization.

Great lecturer, and makes things easy to understand. I was in a class of mostly Japanese lit grad students, but the class was fun and entertaining nonetheless, despite the lack of background I had in Japanese literature.

Proffessor Copeland is great! She's an expert on Japan and makes an introductory course really informative but keeps it interesting by showing videos, slide shows, and varying her lectures. She's also very kind and always willing to help clarify things.

Professor Copeland is an amazing professor. One of my favorite classes I have taken thus far. She is a great lecturer and knows everything there is to know about Japan, plus she lived there for years. I would recommend anything she takes about Japanese civilization. Great!
recommend this class
A really great prof. Nice and enthusiastic.
Presented a lot of good information, but really had a feminist attitude, so a lot of the course focused on women and how badly they're treated in Japan instead of on the beauty of the culture.
Extremely nice, caring professor.  Interested in students' opinions.  Makes the time pass quickly..pretty interesting.
She's pretty lively and interesting.

I loved this course with Professor Marchant so much! If you have any interest in dance, take this course. The professor is so encouraging and makes everyone feel comfortable at the skill level they are at. He promotes an environment where you can learn about modern dance history and explore your body's dance style without judgment.

He's a really cool guy, and makes the class super accessible to non-dancers.  Definitely go in with an open mind, some of the things he says and has you do are a little bit out there, but if you let go of your inhibitions, you get a lot out of the class.  Attendance is mandatory, otherwise, a very easy A, would definitely recommend.

As long as you come to class and appear engaged, this class is an easy A. Marchant is a fantastic person and always interesting. The class may seem a little weird at first but I absolutely loved hearing him explain his perspectives on dance and life. I am not a dancer at all, but I never felt self-conscious.
David is a wonderful dancer and human being. His class is all about exploration and grades you on your personal effort (meaning most students get high marks). I highly recommend this class!

If you really invest yourself in the class and the dance performances on campus, you'll definitely grow to appreciate and understand his unique worldview. A fantastic dancer and a helpful, wonderful teacher. I never once felt self-conscious - he sees beauty and potential in every dancer. Did not play favorites, at least not in our class.
This man is living in his own world. It's funny at first, but after a semester of his theories on existance, you want to shoot yourself. He also seriously plays favorites.

awesome. really laid back.. just show him you are trying and that you care about what he has so say for at least the length of the class. expects nothing more than any one else.. an easy A. (definitely does not play favorites in class)
No Comments
David always seemed open to letting us explore and play in whichever way we seemed fit.  I loved this class and would take it again in a heartbeat.

Rude and forgetful. Told him I had sprained ankle (happened during semester) and he kept telling me to do things he knew I couldn't do b/c of my ankle. I had te remind him about 15 times. Also he totally plays favorites with students.
No Comments
No Comments

Gender, Culture, and Madness was the most interesting class I have ever taken! I looked forward to her lectures and genuinely enjoyed the readings outside of class. She is well spoken, engaging, and clearly an expert at what she does.

Dr. Lester is a good prof because she is very knowledgeable in her professions. Besides that, the slides that she uses to present the material are quite not organized, and you need to try to reorganize yourself and put some effort into making a natural flow of what the content is about. The exams consist of all short answers and essay questions.

Gender, Culture, Madness was probably one of the most interesting classes I've taken. Her slides are created more as talking points for herself instead of notes to copy down. The class was based off of in-person short answer/essay exams that were graded pretty easily. Funny and interesting prof overall.

This class has definitely changed my perspective on mental illness and the health care system. Some pretty intense books were assigned that have made me feel things I never knew I was capable of feeling,  but I immensely enjoyed the classes, 95% of the time. Sometimes we'd have some historical lectures that were boring, but otherwise, it was fun!

I went to class maybe three times this semester. Lester read off her unorganized, unprofessional powerpoints, which she then posted on blackboard. Her papers were unreasonably long, but she told you exactly what to write, which is frustrating. That being said, I got an A without reading any books and studying for about 45 minutes total.

Do not take Rebecca Lester's classes. Unless you're intrigued by unprofessional powerpoints full of incorrect spellings, abbreviations, and random unorganized chaos. The class was easy enough but she does not have anything together.

She is fabulous. Our exams were basically 6-10 pages of writing, which she graded really easily despite the considerable work that goes into them. Discussions are engaging and her work is really interesting as well.
Dr. Lester is great! I had a great time in her class. It's really interesting, and the grading is completely fair. She cares a lot about students and is fun to talk to.

I really like her as a person and a teacher, and the information covered in class is pretty interesting.  However I really don't like the tests. The mult choice are unneccessarily tricky and the other sections are graded way too harshly.  I have a C+ in this class which is my lowest grade ever in college and I do the readings.
Lester is a super nice professor w/ fascinating research.  Obviously intelligent, she needs to learn to not rely on powerpoints b/c they sort of take away from the class.
Great teacher.  Really cares about students, very clear lecturer, and extremely intelligent.

Her part of medicine and society was really interesting. We read a bunch of great books. She is really interested in her work and a pretty laid back teacher. I had a good time in this class and I would recommend medicine and society to every freshman.

Absolutely loved Dr. Skemer's lectures! Always very clear, organized, and engaging. Not a light courseload - lectures are dense, and midterms are content-heavy + a cummulative final - but the professors do everything they can to support students. Interesting and fun. Great intro to EEPS, but you do have to work pretty hard for an A.

It's really sad, because the class is on an interesting subject, but it is just so unnecessarily hard. It's like the TAs try to find something wrong with every one of your answers on the tests. This is my major, yet the class makes me hate it. Worst grade I've ever gotten in college. This is why people hate STEM. Cool stuff, but horribly taught.

Took this class as many other engineers have, to fulfil the upper level science requirement. For a topic that I have zero interest in, Phil and Jen are pretty entertaining, but that's about where it ends. They said that this course isn't a memorization course... It definitely is. Make sure you take good notes because the slides are  very brief.

He gives frat boy in an endearing way. Funny and decent enough lecturer. The class content is just boring and too much for anyone to memorize, especially if you're a humanities major used to writing papers rather than memorizing types of rocks. The exam grades in this class were the lowest I've ever gotten. Difficult to access help outside class.
Great professor and class

Phil tries his best to make lectures entertaining but the real downfall of the class is the material. It is just hard to make 14 billion years of rocks interesting. Unless you want to be a geologist I would not take this class, it does not fall under an intro level course in my opinion.

He's definitely gives good lectures and truly knows the content, but outside of the lectures he is sort of arrogant. I feel like he teaches just to be right about something, he doesn't actually want us to learn. You can definitely tell he never struggled in a class before, and expects everyone to retain the information on the first try.

Deliberately rude, in emails and during class. Deleted Zoom recordings because he said only synchronous students present should have access to content. Very unaccommodating for COVID-19. Gave EC to photograph WashU building (only available to on-campus students) even in a fully remote class. Exams were graded arbitrarily by TAs, no clear criteria.

I had Phil for Geology Bootcamp. I loved every second of that class, mostly because of him and Alex (the other professor). The two of them were like a comedy duo, constantly cracking jokes with one another. You can tell that they're super passionate about geology and love teaching it. They're also very personable and overall fun guys.

Phil is very passionate about rocks and the environment. Even if this subject isn't your life, he will make it interesting purely based on how excited he gets during lectures. He is very straight forward and his tests are based on lectures and labs. The tests are not hard. He gives pop quizzes, but they' re usually two questions and are easy points
He's the best!
best. class. EVER.

Best Chinese teacher. Very critical but cares very much about her students. Pushes you past your comfort zone and calls on people to participate. Her humor is very sarcastic. If you write characters wrong repeatedly she will call you out. Her methods are very effective and expect to improve in Chinese greatly.

Mu laoshi wasn't a very good instructor to native speakers. She assumed you knew things just because you were from a Chinese background and was often in a bad mood that made her really impatient and me unwilling to ask for help or else face her ridicule. If you didn't get it the first time, she probably wouldn't explain it again. Did not enjoy her.

Mu Laoshi is basically amazing. She really makes you learn Chinese well, and makes it a fun experience. She does put you on the spot pretty often, but that only makes you learn better. She has a good sense of humor, too.
Makes class fun, be ready to work

Her knowledge of how to teach non-native speakers Chinese is evident, but she is not for the faint of heart.  Meeker students will DEFINITELY be intimidated by her aggressive style of teaching.  You are put on the spot A LOT and failure can be embarrasing.  If you can handle it, she is awesome.  Outstanding sense of humor if you can laugh along.
She is great, just work hard and Chinese will be a breeze
Hottest personality on the planet
Great teacher. Fun and keeps the class exciting and interesting
No Comments

Prof. Mu is an amazing language professor.  Used to teach at Middlebury, and her knowledge of teaching languages is very evident.  She knows what she is talking about, is very smart, and very clear about concepts.

I've taken Chinese from at least 10 different professors here and in China, she is by far the best Chinese teacher I've had.  Very hard and very demanding, but that's what you need when you're learning a language.  She'll beat the language into you.  Also a great person outside of class.

Michael is first and foremost a stone cold fox: a sexpot piece of man candy. He's also convinced he's Art's gift to God. Most of the time he just can't be bothered with us lesser mortals. Just act like you're just as hot-shit as he is and that you don't care that much and he's all yours. But really, he's got it where it counts: brains and beauty.

MJ balanced on one foot in the gray area of professionalism and casual love child. He spread his penny wisdom like a country crock movie star. Michael was one of my advisors at WashU.  I found him to be extremely knowledgeable about art, and very helpful is assisting me in placing my work within the context of the contemporary art world.  In fact, Michael was the reason I chose WashU grad school over other programs because of his art background.  He is an invaluable part of the program.

Byron will invest in you as you invest in him.  He expects rigor and thought, and he imparts the essential information needed for growth, even if his words are few.  Students that are too self-involved or prideful to listen may miss out on what Byron has to offer.  Then one or two might come here, to work out their demons with an insulting fiction.
Seemed aloof in conversation, and unintrested in his sudents success.

When he was around to give comments, they were actually pretty helpful. excellent

He's a sweetheart. He didn't put up with slackers or cheaters, but if you showed him you cared/were actually trying, he would go to the ends of the earth to help you understand the material.
This guy literally went out of his way to lower the GPA. He was fairly helpful if you met with him, but if not then you'll just remember him as mean-spirited.
Chill teacher, ultra smart, explains stuff well, easy grader? What's not to love?

He's clearly off the charts intelligent and really good at computer science, which is cool but made it difficult to follow him for the first half of the class. He got much, much better for the second half. Overall, I think he did a really good job for his first time teaching and I could definitely seem him as a professor down the line.

he's one of those teachers you don't appreciate until much later when you realize that he's the only reason you understand java at all. also, while he pretends to be strict and tough, he's actually a teddy bear and is nice and understanding about pretty much everything.

Incredible teacher. Hands down. I always thought I'd hate everything about coding until I took this class. Vinoo is an excellent lecturer and really explains concepts well. I've also never seen someone work harder for their students or care more about their students (all while he's balancing his own work). He's great!

Im pretty sure he's a TA (or at least was), but he was awesome in CS 1. He held the best review sessions, explained concepts clearly, and was really understanding and flexible. Sometimes he had to be strict which scared some people, but he was always willing to help and was on top of everything.

Vinoo is actually one of the best professors on this campus. He communicates content and material really well and is very invested in his students. Wash U should really try and retain him (full time) after he graduates.
He was mean spirited and was especially mean to certain members of our class.
Super helpful, but really rips on my buddy Sam for his lack of style in coding

He may be just an undergrad, but that's actually his biggest asset.  He knows what it's like to be a student.  He knows how to test effectively and make assignments of a reasonably difficulty level.  At first I was upset to learn that I would not be taught my a real professor, but Vinoo's lecture style is engaging and effective.

Professor Kelly's lectures are a bit boring but contain interesting information. He does not lecture most of this class. Outside lecturers teach. Even if you do not like one of them, you will like the next one. Professor Kelly will post PowerPoint slides and notes to help you. There is no textbook, so go to class to hear the information.
Cahokia Freshman Seminar is very very easy! Class runs short everyday, minimal effort required.  Dr Kelly is super chill and you get to go on field trips.  Would recommend.

Extremely hard to study for exams and the TA's are absolutely horrible at taking lecture notes. The course composes of guest lecturers, but it's extremely difficult to get lecture slides from them. Unless you're wide awake and jotting down their lecture for 1.5 hour, you will not pass this class.

Good professor - seems to really care about his students. The class consisted almost entirely of guest lecturers and was kind of a mixed bag (one lecture would be extremely interesting to me while the next would be a bit dull), but I would still recommend the class.

Professor Kelly doesn't teach many of the classes (there are guest lecturers), but when he does he is clear and engaging. The class itself is difficult to study for since each class there is a different lecturer, but the topics are extremely interesting and it's a lot of fun to hear about archaeological research.

I had him for the Cahokia Freshman Seminar class. The lectures tend to be drawn out, especially if you're not really into archaeology (I am an anthro major). There are minimal assignments, and he's available to help with them all!

There was a different lecturer for each class, so he only taught 2 or 3 of them. His personal teaching style is fine, but the class was boring overall, and he was very bad at getting the readings up on time. If you like actually being able to study for the exams you have to take, I wouldn't recommend this class. (Pro: he gives extra credit.)
He's the nicest professor I've had at Wash U. and an expert on Cahokia. Definetely take his Cahokia class: it's an easy 'A' and you'll learn so much. He can be a little boring, but it's worth it.
N/A
The class was boring.  Probably my least favorite of the semester...

EXTREMELY opinionated and very liberal, which usually I don't mind. However, she does not like opposition towards her beliefs. She will actually berate students who challenge her views in front of the class. This does not produce good dialogue and made me want to skip class frequently.
I recommend this class. A great perspective for all history majors. A narrative that sadly is all too often lost in American history.  She is a helpful professor and very willing to work with you.

I loved professor Friedman! She was a great lecturer...very clear, always willing to help students, always concerned with students comprehension of the material. She is very opinionated, but if you disagree with her, she is always up for a good debate. She made a 3.5 hour class really fun, brining in movies & food. If you can take this class, do!

She is probably the worst professor i've had at washU. She's very close-minded, really liberal, really wants you to agree with her. if you present an opinion that she doesn't agree with she'll shut you down in class, and not only that, your grade will not reflect your work, it reflects how much she likes you. just a really biased professor.

What a wonderful instructor! Had a seminar which led to my dissertation topic. She's been nothing but friendly and supportive since. Yes, she has high expectations but only because she wants students to realize their full protential--a fact not always recognized by every student! But draw your own conclusions....
Hard on grammer in papers, but generally a fair grader
Great Professor. She was very helpful. Only problem was the class was 3 hours long. There was a lot of reading, but it was very interesting.
Hard grader.  Assigns a lot of reading but on interesting topics.  Long class periods can be boring.

I felt grading was very unfair and that I truly deserved a better grade than I received.  Class was somewhat dull and boring.  I had to take an American History class, but I definitely wish I hadn't chosen this one.
writing intensive class...she was very helpful. only problem was the fact it was three hours long, once a week, with too much reading. discussions were interesting, so were the paper topics.

This was an awful class with an awful teacher if you could even call her that. she doesn't know the subjects even after years of teaching the same content she still has to rely on an answer key to work through the in class problems. She is not helpful when you have questions about the content at all! Never take her if you can help it!

She's a great person, fun to talk to, funny, and genuinely cares about her students. She's flexible and open to suggestions and feedback with her teaching style, too. She does lecture insanely fast, though, and your grade is essentially made up entirely of exam performance. Overall, I think she is great and definitely over-hated.
During our lessons, the exercises she solved were straightforward. However, the exams were unexpectedly harder than she had prepared us for.
Does not know how to teach. Moves way too fast on slides during lecture. Students are left completely clueless which leads to awkward silence frequently
She is horrible. Read slides verbatim. Doesn't understand whats she is teaching. clueless....
Awful

The material itself is not difficult; you'll easily get A if u work on it. No regular homework. She doesn't know how to teach and cannot even explain clearly to easy questions.

Professor Rabier simply does NOT care about her students. The material is not difficult at all, yet she finds a way to make the exams unclear. Shes the most condescending Professor Ive ever had, and she cant even answer students simple question. All she does is ready off the PowerPoint.

Great with emails. But I don't like her teaching style, need to spend other time to watch pre-class lectures and spend lots of time just doing practice problems which is not the way I prefer to learn. Will not take her class again

Her teaching method sucks with the style of having students watch lectures before class and doing practice problems in class. The content on her exam is not straightforward to the stuff you do in class. They are significantly harder.

MJ really cares about students and wants to be there for us personally. Really nice woman. However, I don't like the "flipped" classroom style with asynchronous lecture recordings and then the class is working on practice problems. Homework is optional, only grades are exams. Exams are straightforward. Good professor, bad class.

Amazing, caring professor. Lin laoshi is incredibly dedicated to her students, ensuring they understand the technical nuances with Mandarin and strengthen their language capabilities. Entertaining and really funny in the classroom. A strong asset to the outstanding Chinese dept. at WashU.

Lin Laoshi is the best teacher I've had at WashU so far. She not only kept me engaged in class, but helped me improve my Chinese so much! She's always willing to help if you ask for it and she's a fair grader. There's a decent amount of homework, but it's not too bad. You'll likely get an A if you just put in the effort.
If you have the option to have her as a teacher, take her!
By far the best Chinese100 and Chinese 200 teacher.  Take her!
Awesome prof.  Uses the telesis.  You'll learn a lot and have fun.  Take her Chinese class while you can.
Lin Laoshi is great. Makes Chinese fun and has a sense of humor.
Great teacher.  Really knows what she's doing and is very organized.  Also enjoys humor.

Lin Laoshi is all right, though she is kind of out of touch with what is appropriate to say to students.  She gets embarrassed by flattery, which quickly becomes everyone's goal.  She's an easier grader than Chen, and I think harder than Wang.  Overall, it's a pretty nice class.  Lin Laoshi has a lot of quirks and likes to wear the color black.
Great teacher!
I love her. She's a great teacher, very helpful, and fun.

Definitely my favorite professor I've had so far at WashU. Clearly has a strong passion for the material and is always willing to help if you need it. Recognizes that students have different strengths and weaknesses. Great to talk to during office hours and is very understanding. Spends a lot of time thinking of which books to read and it shows.

I really loved having Prof. Brown this semester! I have no background at all with Caribbean Lit and I found the course very informative and challenging in a good way. I will say he is a stickler for paper copies, so be sure to have one.

An absolutely fantastic professor. He's funny, encourages great discussion, and gives the best feedback out of any professor I've had at WashU. I've already taken 2 courses with him, and I will take more if given the opportunity
Definitely fosters good discussion in class, and the workload always felt very manageable.
A great teacher! Sometimes he only agrees with his own opinion, but he is super open to other opinions still. As long as you are invested he is a great grader & teacher.

Great professor. Less structured than other english professors (who aren't a structured group themselves) but he understands his subject matter and cares about his students. Students who benefit from strict lectures and teacher-led discussion might find his class, in which students lead the discussion more than the professor, challenging.

Literally no lectures.  We read these amazing Caribbean literature works with no context. I feel like a missed out on so much in this course because he never gave us any history about the Caribbean islands or cultural points of reference. However, participation wasn't mandatory and the books are amazing.
Great professor, really helps students improve their writing.

Unproductive class discussions. The class dynamic was fun, but he did not come up with questions that furthered any discussion. Participation is important. Really specific essay formatting and structure, but after the first essay he explains what he is looking for, and if you follow his structure exactly, then your grade will improve.

Professor Brown was a pretty good teacher. Caribbean Literature is definitely foreign for some, so he was really good about giving background information and making things clear. He sprung a pop quiz on the class during the craziest week of midterms season because he wasn't sure if we were paying attention, and he was sadly right. Overall, great.

She is so so funny and makes lectures super interesting and fun. Even though participation/attendance wasn't mandatory, I never wanted to miss a class because I often felt inspired by the art she would teach and the art movements she would delve into. Super good at making connections between things and explaining art in funny/meaningful ways.

Such a person may appear disinterested or indifferent towards their responsibilities as an art history professor, and may prioritize their own sense of humor over providing meaningful critique and guidance. This behavior may lead to a lack of respect from students and undermine the credibility of the academic institution. (This is art)

Professor Sheren's Latinx Art class held many many pros than it did cons. While you read a lot, and participation is required, it all adds up beneficially in the end. You have one project/paper all semester, and you do it bit by bit which makes it easier. Profesor Sheren is also just a delight to talk and discuss with. Her passion shows.

She is probably one of my favorite people, let alone professors, at WashU! To be honest, contemporary art is not really my thing, but I still enjoyed class with her immensely. She's also adapted very well to an online format! She was always easy to reach despite being remote and the assignments were really creative and fun. Would highly recommend!

Very caring and supportive. She gives a lot to read and requires you a good command of the readings. But she is very approachable and always willing to help. Her feedback is very detailed and constructive.

One of the Professors I respect the most at WashU. Learned so much in her seminar, and looking back she really taught IMAAD differently/more critically than many profs would. Lots of work and participation is required, grades fairly. Super helpful and accommodating professor and extremely knowledgable!

My favorite art history prof at WashU. She makes you work to get good grades, but at the same time will work with you if you need accommodations/extensions. Lots of assigned reading that you can get away without doing, although it'll be harder to participate. I learned so much in Ila's class and recommend her if you're serious about art history.
It is a good lecture to fall asleep except the chairs are uncomfortable.
Hate her lectures. She really thinks herself hilarious but actually cold and awkward. She speaks too fast and not to the point and never fails to make you fall asleep.

She's not really a stand-out professor. Her lectures are interesting and she's engaging but she gets annoyed really easily. She has her TA's take phones from students and she's always berating the class to be quiet. Granted, it's not too difficult and you don't really need to read for class. The other teacher (Klein) is more boring but more tame

Prof. Martin, while sometimes difficult to understand, is very motivated about teaching accounting. Her tests aren't the easiest, but quizzes tend to be very easy. She doesn't assign homework, but the recommended problems definitely help.

An excellent teacher. Well explained, interesting material, and fair tests. Plus if you participate in class she gives out candy. The course is curved too so that's a nice addition too. Attend class and do the practice problems and it's an easy A, regardless of what lazy B Schoolers may say.

Very nice woman, but the class did not take her seriously and she had no control. It was also hard to understand her with her accent, but what can you do. Not a terribly hard class, but it's easy to get caught not going to class and falling behind.
cant speak english

Can be hard to understand at times...lots of time wasted in class due to clarity issues. Looking over the slides in my room at night proved more useful than going to class. Go to class if you want, but it's not necessary.

I really liked prof. Martin. Her class was pretty boring, but accounting in general is pretty boring. I didn't find her very hard to understand, more that she would ask obvious and sometimes pointless questions. But in the end her tests were easier, the grades turned out well and I learned the material really well.

I was at first skeptical about how this class would unfold after reading comments from prior semesters. However, this semester was a pleasant surprise. Prof Martin is always prepared, and she finds a way to teach the material in a way that you can understand very quickly. You really only need the book for the first few chapters.
Prof. Martin is very knowledgeable and a great teacher overall...she is willing to help her students and offers plenty of opportunities for extra credit.  Strongly recommend the class...

Professor Martin may at first come off as difficult to understand, but once you really begin to talk to her you'll find out she really cares about her students and will work hard to help you succeed. She does have an accent, but I think, as long as you read the textbook, the information is still clear. She is a fair grader and nice!

This teacher shamelessly plagiarizes the work of other ACCT profs simply to minimize her own effort.  She takes PPT slides from other professors and reorganizes them so they don't appear identical; the result is an incoherent, disorderly presentation that she can't explain because she DIDN'T write it.  She is HARDLY a professor worth taking.

Very nice and very helpful. The entire course is organized extremely well, which makes learning content and keeping up with things much easier. Content seems hard at first but is presented in an easily digestible way. Exams are fair and you are allowed cheat sheets. Genuinely seemed to care about her students in and out of the classroom.
Amazing professor. Most organized professor, teaches important concepts that would help outside class too. Highly reccomend.

One of my favorite classes taken at washu. Lectures can sometimes get a bit boring, but if you follow along with her notes, it makes the problems on homeworks and exams way more doable. In class, she tells you exactly what she is looking for in grading, and she cares about learning more than grades. The material we learned was very practical.

I really like how this class was set up. The expectations were all set and the grading was super cut and clean. I love how she does the problems in class. We learned coding this sem with anaconda. That was super fun. Overall do well on the homework and exams. There are a lot of TA office hours, which was nice. She is the best!

Forough is one of my favorite professors I have had so far at WashU! This is not a hard class. Exams are weighted so that your worst score is only worth about 17% of your grade. Yes, the problem sets can be a bit tricky but if you take advantage of office hours you will be fine. The class was very interesting, we learned Excel Solver and SQL.
She's clearly a nice person, knowledgeable, and grading was very fair, but she's a boring lecturer.

Does not post annotated lecture slides which makes doing the problem sets very difficult as you have to go back and rewatch hour and a half long lecture videos. Problem sets are also a large percentage of grade which makes it frustrating when it's hard to find the notes from class. Also goes very quickly in class which makes her hard to follow.
She is very nice and clearly cares about the subject material. However, not engaging during class: speaks very softly, doesn't repeat herself, and asks questions in the form of statements.

I really liked Prof. Ahangar. I was prepared to dislike her because I hate statistics and usually don't do well, but I enjoyed this class. If you pay attention and do the homework, you'll be good. I half-paid attention in class and did well w/ homework and did average. She has a dry sense of humor & you can tell that she cares about her students (:

I really liked her class because I could tell how knowledgeable she is. This was her first semester and she adapted to students' complaints when they did poorly on homework and exams simply because they didn't pay attention in class. Her practice exams were similar to the real exam, all based on the homework assignments we had.

Dude is just a 10/10 chiller. Don't get me wrong, great philosophers is not a class I enjoy, but Dr. K makes up for it. I had some really important meetings I needed to attend during class hours, and he was super understanding - something I can't say for others. I love this guy even if his class isn't my thing.

Business Ethics was by far my favorite class that i've taken. All the readings were super interesting and the workload was very easy. A quiz before every class but graded on completion, 2 short essay exams(notes/readings allowed), and 1 short paper. It's a fascinating topic and he makes discussion flow very smoothly.
Nic is a fun lecturer that makes class pretty engaging. The class (business ethics) was very chill, but attendance is required.

Good at philosophy, but would talk for an extended period. The course was very disorganized, and he changed the grading requirements a few times. He's a kind person, and holds great office hours, but has really weird paper requirements. His style is unique, which could be good or bad depending on the person. Overall, good professor.

As my first philosophy course, I wasn't ready for the density of the material, but his explanations made the class possible for me. Without him walking us through each reading from before the class, I would not have gotten an A. The amount of reading tended to fluctuate, but it got harder as the semester went on.
Gives huge mansplainer vibes and definitely loves the sound of his own voice. But, at the same time, very thoughtful and intelligent. Also very kind and understanding.

I cannot say enough good things about Professor Koziolek. He is absolutely brilliant, but what truly sets him apart is the way he runs his class; he encourages us to openly embrace our mistakes, and is very humble in encouraging us to question and doubt the things he says. Highly recommend taking a class of his during your time at WashU.

Prof. Koziolek was a really great teacher and an interesting lecturer. The class grade depended heavily on essays, with a little bit for participation. There were quizzes on each reading, but they were graded on completion. I highly recommend going to him about the essays since they are such a big part of the grade.

Best prof I've ever had. He cares about each individual student, is exceptionally kind and understanding, and facilitates learning in a non-stressful way. He's wonderfully human in the way that he understands and relates to students, creating a safe space. Not to mention absolutely brilliant. I love the guy and you'd be lucky to learn from him:)
Fantastic lecturer, extremely knowledgable, hilarious person. Talk with him outside of class just to hear what he has to sayyou will not regret it.

Nic is a super cool guy. Go to office hours - discussing things w/ him 1-on-1 is wonderful. Is very well read in philosophy and will engage you on the readings. You could probably not read in-depth and still do fine, but reading carefully made the class more engaging. Lectures can be dry sometimes, but the concepts covered are all interesting.
I loved this course and Dean McClelland taught it wonderfully. You have to write and take many smaller quizzes but nothing is too intensive and class discussions are always fun.

This might be a hot take, but I think every WashU student should take a class with Prof. McClelland. She's a dynamic lecturer, genuinely cares about what her students think, and has an infectious enthusiasm for what she teachers. She makes classics accessible! Beyond all that, she's a good person to know on campus and a wonderful person all around.

Love Dean McClelland so much! So incredibly caring and very close to students. Her lectures are engaging, and while there is a lot of reading and papers, it isn't very hard work or graded harshly. Make sure to participate for full points, but that means showing up and speaking once or twice a class. Would highly recommend!

Professor McClelland is one of the best professors I've ever met at WashU. She is so caring and understanding, you can tell that she wants all of her students to succeed. She's one of WashU's gems, take a class with her if you have the chance! You do have to read for the exams/quizzes, but the readings are so interesting that it's worth it. 11/10
Prof. McClelland is great. Her lectures are engaging and she's a good teacher overall. Very approachable and down to earth, not intimidating but still very much respected.

I've had professor McClelland for three classes, including her film class, and she's been awesome for all three. She has realistic expectations for her students and never fails to make class interesting. She also allowed me to do an extra assignment after a semester ended to bring up my grade in a class from that semester. All around awesome prof!!

Dr. McClelland wants you to learn. She says on the first day that she doesn't care about grades, and that is the truth. She will work with you until you succeed. The lectures are interesting and the papers are pretty straightforward. I put it 3 difficulty because it requires a lot of time needed outside of class to watch movies and read books.
Loved this class! Super interesting topic with an easy-going professor. Grizelda is very engaging and dynamic. Easy to get a good grade without a tremendous workload.
Hands down the best course I've taken at WashU so far - such an interesting class, and she really helps you get into the topic. 10/10 would recommend!
Such an amazing teacher, amazing class. A lot of reading, but you learn so much.

This professor was THE worst Spanish professor I had at WashU. He never responded to emails, returned assignments months later, and gave minimal feedback to improve overall work in the class. I am a quieter student and although I received A's on all of my assignments, I still ended up getting an A-. Upon questioning the grade, I got 0 response.

Taken a few classes with him, enjoyed them all. The pattern is: grade mostly based a few major assignments but he gives you info to prepare and is pretty lenient. He'll work with you and also if you're interested in a particular topic he will be very enthusiastic to help you learn more.

Only wouldn't take if you have no interest in linguistics.

The class was very enjoyable. It did require a lot of memorization, but overall I thought the work load was reasonable. Overall, Professor Barcroft was a great professor and I would recommend his class to other advanced Spanish students interested in linguistics.
Highly recommended. He knows what he's doing. And he is very helpful.

Prof. Barcroft is a great professor! He's really nice and its obvious that he has a real passion and love for his subject. The class though is pretty difficult and he assigns a ton of homework. He gives you a study guide for every test though so there are no surprises come exam day. Overall, I'd definitely recommend him.

He's an excellent professor and it is clear that he knows the material.  His passion for the material is also evident.  He is very helpful and makes sure that you understand the material.  Also, you get study guides for the exams, so you don't have a reason to score poorly.  Interesting subject matter and you learn a lot!
A good professor with a clear understanding of Spanish linguistic and a helpful attitude.
An approachable and easygoing professor with an obvious love for the subject matter.
Just ask for help if needed, he's more than willing to help you out with school work and department stuff.  Nice guy.
